summary: Joel describes how FogBUGZ originally shipped with a restrictive license preventing reverse engineering or source code modification. When customers asked about source licenses for customization, Joel realized there was no good reason for the restriction. He immediately changed the license to be more permissive, and uses this as a launching point for a longer essay about the value of open source code access.
key_points: Default restrictive software licenses often exist without any clear business justification
Listening to honest customer requests can reveal flawed assumptions in your business practices
When you can't think of a reason for a restriction, removing it is the right call
Openness with source code can be a competitive advantage rather than a liability
money_quotes: Why *does* the license say you can't change the source code? I couldn't think of a single reason.
In fact I thought of a lot of counter-reasons, and immediately changed the license agreement.
Various honest people have asked how much we charge for a source license, so that they could customize a few things.
key_insight: If you can't articulate a reason for restricting your customers, the restriction shouldn't exist.
